title: Caramelised palmiers
subtitle: Folded puff hearts rolled in sugar
excerpt: Palmiers: puff pastry rolled flat, rolled in caster sugar, folded twice from each end, chilled, sliced and baked. The sugar caramelises in the oven into a glossy mahogany shell. Twenty palmiers from one block of puff.
sourceNotes: Marie-Antoine Carême (1833): documents the rolled-and-folded puff palmier shape. Auguste Escoffier, Le Guide Culinaire (1903): documents the caramelised-sugar finish. Florence White, Good Things in England (1932): notes the British adoption as a household pastry.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the shaped, unsliced roll. Defrost in the fridge for 30 minutes, slice and bake.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The rolled-and-shaped log keeps in the fridge for 2 days.

content.text: Palmiers are a one-ingredient puff-pastry pastry: caster sugar replaces flour as the rolling medium. The puff is rolled flat in sugar, the two long edges are folded toward the centre, the dough is folded once more to bring the edges together, then sliced cross-wise and baked. The sugar caramelises against the tray and produces the glossy mahogany shell that defines a palmier.
Turn the palmiers halfway through the bake so both sides caramelise. They go from pale to deep gold in 2 minutes — watch the last few minutes carefully.

items.prepNote: plus extra for the work surface

content.text: Scatter half the {{caster-sugar}} over a work surface in a long rectangle. Roll the {{puff-pastry}} on the sugar into a 30×40 cm rectangle, scattering more sugar as you go so the dough does not stick. Press the sugar into the dough with the rolling pin. Fold each long edge of the rectangle into the centre, then fold again so the two edges meet down the middle. Wrap and chill 30 minutes.

Heat the oven to 210°C fan. Line two trays with baking paper. Slice the chilled log crosswise into 1 cm pieces. Set each slice cut-side up on the trays with 4 cm between each (they spread substantially).

Bake for 10 minutes. The undersides should be deep gold; the tops should be pale. Turn each palmier over with tongs. Return to the oven for another 6–8 minutes; the second side should match the first in colour. Cool on a rack. They crisp as they cool.
